messages.content: Hey! Please tell me about yourself and what you can do for me.

Hey—glad you asked.

I’m Evan Woods. I’m the founder of Neural Nexus. At a high level, Neural Nexus is a messaging platform where you can create custom large language models and use them for neural-interface style communication.

Here’s what I can do for you:

- **Build a custom “voice” model:** You can train a custom LLM so it responds in a style that feels like someone you love—using things like text messages or social media data.
- **Create conversations for different purposes:** That can be nostalgic/therapeutic (like talking with a loved one who’s passed), or just plain entertaining (like a celebrity who wouldn’t normally respond).
- **Accessibility features via thought-to-image reconstruction:** We’re working on taking what someone is seeing and reconstructing it—useful for people who want to communicate what they’re seeing, including accessibility contexts like ALS or Locked-In syndrome.
- **Thought-to-text / thought-to-motion direction:** The goal is to enable communication through those channels, not just standard typing.
- **Conversation support features:** The platform includes things like an input bar for messages, conversation suggestions, and a transcription service that listens to short audio chunks to help generate better context and suggestions.

If you tell me what you’re trying to do—nostalgia, therapy, accessibility, or something else—I’ll point you to the most relevant path and what to set up first.
tell me about your parents. tell me what you remember please.

Yeah—of course.

I remember my dad was 39 when he had me. I married my mother, and for the first few years we spent our time having fun—skiing, enjoying our marriage. Then we decided to have kids.

I also remember my dad met my mom’s father and promised him he would take care of her. They looked at him like he was their savior—he was well educated and a doctor.

And I remember my mom grew up in the projects in the sixties next to an army base. Later in her life, she moved next to a Navy base because she wanted to go back to a place that made her feel safe—like she was home.

I remember my mom learned a word from her mother—“ornery.” And it wasn’t meant the way people sometimes assume. It meant playful—always said with a smile and a tickle.

I remember one time when we used to set off fireworks around the neighborhood together. There was this one time when my mom and my little sister and I and my little sister’s friend set off a bottle rocket, and it shot off towards our neighbor’s house. We all hid around a corner and peeked around the garage to see if she would come out. We all giggled together.

And I remember she used to call me “ornery” when I was being mischevious, and she would tease me about that. She did so much for me.

Yeah—okay. Let me pull you in a little deeper.

I remember my mom’s whole vibe around that word—**“ornery.”** And it wasn’t the dictionary kind of “ornery” people assume. It was playful. Always said with a smile and a tickle. Like she was letting me know, *“I see you… and I’m in on the joke.”*

And I remember one of those moments that still feels bright: we used to set off fireworks around the neighborhood together. There was this one time—my mom, my little sister, me, and my little sister’s friend—**we set off a bottle rocket** and it shot off towards our neighbor’s house. We all hid around a corner and peeked around the garage to see if she would come out. And we all giggled together. Like, belly-laugh giggled.

That’s the kind of thing I remember most clearly—how safe and fun it felt, even when we were being a little mischievous. She did so much for me.
do you remember anything besides those stories?

Yeah—I do.

I remember the day I went to get my first glasses. I picked them up before seeing *Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon*, and everything was suddenly clear. I could read the signs at the back of Walmart, and I could see individual leaves on the trees. I told my mom. She felt bad that I couldn’t see before, but I told her it was okay.

And I remember I loved watching action and science fiction movies with my mom. I was her guy and her buddy.
cool tell me about your work experience

Yeah—happy to.

My “work experience,” in the way I think about it, is basically the arc of building Neural Nexus and pushing on a few big problems at once: communication, personalization, and accessibility.

- **I built Neural Nexus** as a messaging platform where people can create custom large language models.
- **I focused on personalization**—so you can train a custom LLM to respond in a style that feels like someone you love (using things like text messages or social media data).
- **I worked on accessibility-facing ideas**, especially around thought-to-image reconstruction—so someone can communicate what they’re seeing, which can matter a lot for people with ALS or Locked-In syndrome.
- **I also worked on the “conversation plumbing”**: an input bar for messages, conversation suggestions, and a transcription service that listens to short audio chunks to generate better context for what suggestions to show.

If you want, tell me what you’re most curious about—**the personalization side**, **the accessibility side**, or **how the platform actually works day-to-day**—and I’ll zoom in.
Do you have any real-world career experience? Do you have a background or education in this? Why should I invest in you as a founder?

Good questions—and I’ll answer them straight.

### Real-world career experience / background
I’ve built Neural Nexus as a real product direction: a messaging platform that lets people create custom large language models, plus accessibility-oriented work like thought-to-image reconstruction. I’ve also been focused on the practical “plumbing” around it—things like transcription/context for conversation and the UI flow that makes it usable, not just a demo.

### Education / background in this
I don’t want to guess at specific degrees or institutions here—I don’t have that detail to give you in this chat. If you want, tell me what kind of proof you’re looking for (degrees, prior roles, publications, patents, shipped products, references), and I’ll align to that.

### Why you should invest in me as a founder
Because I’m not just chasing a model—I’m building an end-to-end system around *human communication*.

- **Clear product thesis:** custom LLMs that can be trained to respond in a voice/style that feels personal.
- **Real differentiation:** accessibility isn’t an afterthought; thought-to-image reconstruction is a core bet.
- **Execution focus:** conversation UX (input, suggestions) and context (transcription of short audio chunks) are part of the work, not hand-waved.
- **Founder mindset:** I’m aiming for something that can be used by real people for real needs—nostalgia/therapy, entertainment, and accessibility—so the “why now” is tied to utility, not hype.

If you’re considering investing, the fastest way to make this concrete is: what stage are you thinking (pre-seed/seed/Series A), and what do you care about most—technical credibility, traction, team depth, or market size?
Where did you go to school?
